rule.desc: Prefer absolute imports for public modules; use relative imports for private (underscore-prefixed) modules within packages.
rule.when_to_use: - Relative imports: importing `_module.py` from another module in same package - Relative imports: private cross-imports within package implementation - Absolute imports: importing public modules from other packages - Avoid relative imports in top-level scripts outside packages
rule.rationale: Relative imports for private modules emphasize package-internal structure and make refactoring easier. They signal that these imports are implementation details, not public dependencies. Absolute imports for public modules make code more portable and explicit.

example.code: # Good - relative import for private module within same package # In src/orders/_service.py from ._repository import OrderRepository from ._models import Order, OrderItem
# Good - absolute import for public module from src.util.database import Connection
# Bad - absolute import for private module in same package from src.orders._repository import OrderRepository # Use relative import instead
# Bad - relative import for public module from ..util.database import Connection # Use absolute import instead

rule.tool_support: ruff I252 can enforce relative import rules. ruff I001-I005 enforce general import style.

rule.desc: Standard library, third-party, local. Alphabetical within groups.
Avoid circular dependencies. Use protocols or TYPE_CHECKING.

examples.example: See PY120-TYPE-CHECKING for avoiding circular imports.

rule.desc: Import at module level by default. Use local imports only for optional dependencies or circular imports.

examples.example: `if TYPE_CHECKING: from expensive_module import Heavy` to avoid runtime cost.

rule.desc: For untyped third-party libs, use stub files or `py.typed`.
Avoid namespace packages unless distributing plugins.

examples.example: Use regular packages with `__init__.py` for standard projects.
